content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 22, 2015

Jonathan S. Mintzer
The Sutton Law Firm
150 Post Street, Suite 405
San Francisco, CA 94108

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-15-242

Dear Mr. Mintzer:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because
your questions are general in nature and not on behalf of any specific client, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.

DISCUSSION

In 2014, operative July 1, 2014, Section 84222 was added to the Act and sets forth the
criteria for when a Multipurpose Organization ("MPO") becomes a recipient committee. A MPO is
defined as an organization described in Sections 501(c)(3) to 501(c)(10), inclusive, of the Internal
Revenue Code and that is exempt from taxation under Section 501(a) of the Internal Revenue Code.
This definition includes federal or out-of-state political committees, trade and professional
associations, civic and religious organizations, fraternal societies, educational institutions, or any
other association or group of persons acting in concert and operating for purposes other than
making contributions or expenditures. (Section 84222(a).)

Pursuant to Section 84222(c), a MPO is a recipient committee within the meaning of
subdivision (a) of Section 82013 only under one or more of the following circumstances:

"(1) The multipurpose organization is a political committee registered
with the Federal Election Commission, except as provided in subdivision (a) of
this section, or a political committee registered with another state, and the
multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures in this state in an
amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section
82013.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-15-242
Page No. 2

"(2) The multipurpose organization solicits and receives payments from
donors in an amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision
(a) of Section 82013 for the purpose of making contributions or expenditures.²

"(3) The multipurpose organization accepts payments from donors in an
amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section
82013 subject to a condition, agreement, or understanding with the donor that all
or a portion of the payments may be used for making contributions or
expenditures.

"(4) The multipurpose organization has existing funds from a donor and a
subsequent agreement or understanding is reached with the donor that all or a
portion of the funds may be used for making contributions or expenditures in an
amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section
82013. The date of the subsequent agreement or understanding is deemed to be
the date of receipt of the payment.

"(5) The multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures
totaling more than fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) in a period of 12 months or
more than one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) in a period of four
consecutive calendar years.

"(A) A multipurpose organization shall not qualify as a committee within
the meaning of subdivision (a) of Section 82013 pursuant to this paragraph if the
multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures using only
available nondonor funds. A multipurpose organization that makes contributions
or expenditures with nondonor funds shall briefly describe the source of the funds
used on its major donor or independent expenditure report.

"(B) For purposes of this paragraph, 'nondonor funds' means investment
income, including capital gains, or income earned from providing goods, services,
or facilities, whether related or unrelated to the multipurpose organization's
program, sale of assets, or other receipts that are not donations."

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

1. Does Section 84222(c)(5) require a MPO to account for contributions and expenditures
made prior to July 1, 2014 in determining whether it qualifies as a recipient committee?

The effective date of the legislation was July 1, 2014. The statute does not expressly set a
later effective date for compliance. Thus, the plain language requires compliance as of July 1, 2014
and requires contributions and expenditures made prior to July 1, 2014 to be considered in
determining whether the multipurpose organization qualifies as a committee under

² The threshold for qualification as a recipient committee was amended effective January 1, 2016 to $2,000.
(See AB 594 (Gordon), Chapter 364, Statutes of 2015.)

File No. I-15-242
Page No. 3

Section 84222(c). However, donors who gave to the organization prior to July 1, 2014, would not
have to be disclosed as specified in Section 84222(e)(4).

You asked whether this might be considered a retroactive application of the statute. A
statute is not applied retroactive merely because some of the facts upon which its application
depends came into existence before its enactment. (Kizer v. Hanna (1989) 48 Cal.3d 1, 7.)
Moreover, a construction of the statute that clears the slate and restarts day one of the "look-back"
period on the effective date would result in delayed implementation of the new statute for possibly
four years. This result would be contrary to the purposes of the statute and the general purposes of
the Act. Had it been intended that the look-back did not apply to activity before July 1, 2014, that
would have been stated explicitly in the statute as Section 84222(e)(4) explicitly stated for
disclosure of donor information.

If a multipurpose organization qualifies as a committee under Section 84222(c)(5) because it
made contributions or expenditures from its general fund of more than $50,000 in a period of 12
months or more than $100,000 in a period of four consecutive calendar years it will identify its
recent donors using the last in, first out (LIFO) accounting method described in Section 84222(g). If
an organization qualifies as a committee under this section of the Act and is using LIFO to identify
its donors, following Section 84222(e)(4) it will identify donors only back to July 1, 2014, and will
identify the organization itself for any remaining amount of the contribution or expenditure.

2 and 3. May a MPO use "nondonor funds" earned in previous fiscal years to make
contributions and expenditures without qualifying as a recipient committee pursuant to section
84222(c)(5) and which accounting method should a MPO use to demonstrate the availability of
"nondonor funds" for purposes of Section 84222(c)(5)(A)?

Yes. Under Section 84222, a MPO that uses funds received from donors to make
contributions or expenditures in California elections at the specified thresholds qualifies as a
committee required to disclose the donors of the funds used. On the other hand, if a MPO makes
contributions or expenditures in California elections using funds that were not received from
donors, there are no donors to disclose for those transactions. The MPO would file a major donor
report if it made contributions of $10,000 or more, or an independent expenditure report for
expenditures of $1,000 or more. A multipurpose organization that makes contributions or
expenditures with nondonor funds is required to briefly describe the source of the funds used on its
major donor or independent expenditure report. (Section 84222(c)(5)(A).)

Section 84222(c)(5)(B) defines "nondonor funds" as investment income, including capital
gains, or income earned from providing goods, services, or facilities, whether related or unrelated to
the multipurpose organization's program, sale of assets, or other receipts that are not donations. For
example, a MPO may receive income from conference fees, from the sale of a headquarters
building, or from prevailing in litigation. All these sources of income are nondonor funds. An
organization's funds that meet the definition of "nondonor" funds are exempted by Section
84222(c)(5)(A) and (B) no matter when they were received.

The statute does not mandate a specific accounting method for certifying that funds are
nondonor funds. It will be incumbent on the organization to be able to show that contributions or
expenditures claimed to be made with nondonor funds in fact were. Regulation 18422(f) states that

File No. I-15-242
Page No. 4

a "multipurpose organization must maintain all records necessary to establish its compliance with
Section 84222 . . ."

If income to the organization is kept in an account separate from donations, that is the
easiest way to demonstrate that funds are not from donors. Other accounting methods of showing
the source of the funds are also appropriate, however, we cannot advise on what methods can be
used absent specific facts. An approach that inaccurately estimates or inflates the amount of
nondonor funds would not be appropriate. For example, if an organization kept nondonor funds in
its general account mixed with donations, and there has been inflow and outflow from this account
for several years, the organization may not count up all nondonor receipts back through numerous
fiscal years, skip donor funds, and assert that the expenditure was made with nondonor funds.
Basically, the organization must have records or documentation sufficient for auditors to verify that
the source of the funds used was nondonor funds.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

determining whether the multipurpose organization qualifies as a committee under 
2 The threshold for qualification as a recipient committee was amended effective January I, 2016 to $2,000. 
(See AB 594 (Gordon), Chapter 364, Statutes of2015.)  
Section 84222(c). However, donors who gave to the organization prior to July 1, 2014, would not 
have to be disclosed as specified in Section 84222(e)(4). 
You asked whether this might be considered a retroactive application of the statute. A 
statute is not applied retroactive merely because some of the facts upon which its application

four years. This result would be contrary to the purposes of the statute and the general purposes of 
the Act. Had it been intended that the look-back did not apply to activity before July 1, 2014, that 
would have been stated explicitly in the statute as Section 84222(e)(4) explicitly stated for

If a multipurpose organization qualifies as a committee under Section 84222(c)(5) because it

an organization qualifies as a committee under this section of the Act and is using LIFO to identify 
its donors, following Section 84222( e )( 4) it will identify donors only back to July 1, 20 I 4, and will 
identify the organization itself for any remaining amount of the contribution or expenditure. 
2 and 3. May a MPO use "nondonor fimds" earned in previous fiscal years to make 
contributions and expenditures without q11alifyi11g as a recipient committee pursuant to section

If income to the organization is kept in an account separate from donations, that is the

In 2014, operative July I, 2014, Section 84222 was added to the Act and sets forth the 
criteria for when a Multipurpose Organization ("MPO") becomes a recipient committee. A MPO is 
defined as an organization described in Sections 50l(c)(3) to 50l(c)(10), inclusive, of the Internal 
Revenue Code and that is exempt from taxation under Section 50l(a) of the Internal Revenue Code. 
This definition includes federal or out-of-state political committees, trade and professional 
associations, civic and religious organizations, fraternal societies, educational institutions, or any 
other association or group of persons acting in concert and operating for purposes other than 
making contributions or expenditures. (Section 84222(a).) 
Pursuant to Section 84222(c), a MPO is a recipient committee within the meaning of 
subdivision (a) of Section 82013 only under one or more of the following circumstances: 
"(l) The multipurpose organization is a political committee registered 
with the Federal Election Commission, except as provided in subdivision (a) of 
this section, or a political committee registered with another state, and the 
multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures in this state in an 
amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section 
82013. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in"(2) The multipurpose organization solicits and receives payments from 
donors in an amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision 
(a) of Section 82013 for the purpose of making contributions or expenditures.2

amount equal to or 1::,rreater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section 
82013 subject to a condition, agreement, or understanding with the donor that all 
or a portion of the payments may be used for making contributions or 
expenditures. 
"(4) The multipurpose organization has existing funds from a donor and a 
subsequent agreement or understanding is reached with the donor that all or a 
portion of the funds may be used for making contributions or expenditures in an 
amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section 
82013. The date of the subsequent agreement or understanding is deemed to be 
the date of receipt of the payment. 
"(5) The multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures 
totaling more than fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) in a period of J 2 months or 
more than one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) in a period of four 
consecutive calendar years. 
"(A) A multipurpose organization shall not qualify as a committee within 
the meaning of subdivision ( a) of Section 82013 pursuant to this paragraph if the

available nondonor funds. A multipurpose organization that makes contributions 
or expenditures with nondonor funds shall briefly describe the source of the funds 
used on its major donor or independent expenditure report.

income, including capital gains, or income earned from providing goods, services, 
or facilities, whether related or unrelated to the multipurpose organization's 
program, sale of assets, or other receipts that are not donations."
